result:
# The Memory Keeper

The old woman kept memories in glass jars.

Not her own—those were fading, slipping through the cracks in her mind like sand through arthritic fingers. No, she kept other people's memories. The ones they wanted to forget.

Her shop stood on a corner that existed only on Tuesdays and Thursdays, wedged between a laundromat and a store that sold nothing but buttons. A hand-painted sign above the door read "Burden Relief Services," though most people who found it weren't looking for signs. They were looking for relief.

Today, a young man pushed through the door, shoulders hunched against rain that hadn't started falling yet.

"I need to forget," he said, because they always got right to it. No one came here for small talk.

The old woman nodded, her silver hair catching the light from lamps that burned without electricity. "Show me," she said.

He held out his hands, and between them, the memory shimmered into visibility—a technique she'd taught him without words, a knowing that happened the moment he stepped across her threshold. The memory looked like smoke and stained glass, swirling with colors she'd learned to read: deep purple for grief, red for rage, and that particular shade of gray that meant guilt.

"She didn't deserve what I said," he whispered. "My sister. The last words before the accident."

The old woman had heard variations of this story perhaps a thousand times. Last words. Wrong words. Words that crystallized into weapons when time ran out for apologies.

"The forgetting costs something," she reminded him. Always the warning. Always the same tired speech. "When I take the memory, I take its weight, but also its shape. You'll forget the bad, but also the texture of the good wrapped around it. You'll lose the exact shade of her anger, but also the precise tone of her laugh. Memories aren't surgical. They're roots. Pull one, and others come loose."

"I don't care. I can't carry this anymore."

She nodded. They never cared. Not at first.

She reached out with hands that were older than they looked, older perhaps than the building itself, and drew the memory from the space between his palms. It came reluctantly, as they always did, stretched like taffy, clinging to him with desperate fingers. But she was practiced, and it came free with a sound like tearing silk.

The young man gasped—relief, loss, or both. He'd never be able to say which.

The old woman turned to her shelves, lined floor to ceiling with jars of every size. Mason jars, medicine bottles, antique apothecary containers with Latin labels worn smooth. Each one glowed faintly from within, holding its cargo of carefully extracted pain.

She found an empty jar—sea-green glass, the size of her palm—and coaxed the memory inside. It fought, the way they always did, wanting to return home. But she whispered words in a language she didn't remember learning, and it settled, swirling, caught.

"Will you keep it forever?" the young man asked.

"Until you're ready to take it back."

"I'll never be ready."

"They all say that," she said, not unkindly. "My shelves tell a different story."

He left lighter, buoyant almost, already forgetting the weight of what he'd carried. Already forgetting her face, the shop, how he'd gotten here. By the time he reached the street, he'd remember only that he'd been running an errand, though he couldn't recall what kind.

Alone again, the old woman sat in her chair—the one that had molded itself to her shape over decades or perhaps centuries—and held the jar up to the light.

Inside, she could see everything. The sister's face, sharp with anger. The brother's words, flung like stones. The squeal of tires. The silence after. But also: birthday candles and shared secrets, inside jokes and parallel scars from learning to ride bikes together, the complicated geometry of growing up in the same small house.

She set the jar on the shelf between a bottle containing a mother's postpartum depression and a vial holding a soldier's frozen moment of cowardice.

Her shelves were very full.

Sometimes people came back. Not often, but sometimes. Years later, decades later, they'd push through her door with a confused expression, drawn by something they couldn't name. They'd look at the shelves, and their eyes would catch on a particular jar, and they'd say, "That's mine, isn't it?"

And she'd nod and take it down, and ask, "Are you ready?"

Sometimes they were. Sometimes they'd remember just enough of the shape of their loss to want it back, weight and all. She'd open the jar, and the memory would rush home like a salmon upstream, and they'd stagger under the sudden freight of feeling. But they'd stand straighter too. More complete. More themselves.

Sometimes they'd look at their jar and say, "Not yet," and leave.

And sometimes—this was rarer—they'd look at the shelves upon shelves of glowing jars and ask, "Don't you get tired? Keeping all this pain?"

The old woman would smile then, the way cliffs smile at the ocean.

"Someone has to hold it," she'd say. "Someone has to remember that forgetting is borrowed, not bought. Someone has to keep the door open for when you're strong enough to be whole again."

Outside, the rain began to fall, right on schedule. The old woman made tea in a pot older than memory and sat among her shelves of beautiful, terrible light, keeping watch over sorrows that weren't hers but were her responsibility nonetheless.

On Wednesdays and Fridays, the shop wasn't there at all.

But it was always waiting.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
